html:
XXIX

Ascend the watchtower yonder, valiant soldier,
Look on the field, and say how goes the battle.

Schiller‚Äôs Maid of Orleans

A moment of peril is often also a moment of openhearted kindness and affection. We are thrown off our guard by the general agitation of our feelings, and betray the intensity of those, which, at more tranquil periods, our prudence at least conceals, if it cannot altogether suppress them. In finding herself once more by the side of Ivanhoe, Rebecca was astonished at the keen sensation of pleasure which she experienced, even at a time when all around them both was danger, if not despair. As she felt his pulse, and enquired after his health, there was a softness in her touch and in her accents implying a kinder interest than she would herself have been pleased to have voluntarily expressed. Her voice faltered and her hand trembled, and it was only the cold question of Ivanhoe, ‚ÄúIs it you, gentle maiden?‚Äù which recalled her to herself, and reminded her the sensations which she felt were not and could not be mutual. A sigh escaped, but it was scarce audible; and the questions which she asked the knight concerning his state of health were put in the tone of calm friendship. Ivanhoe answered her hastily that he was, in point of health, as well, and better than he could have expected‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄúThanks,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúdear Rebecca, to thy helpful skill.‚Äù

‚ÄúHe calls me dear Rebecca,‚Äù said the maiden to herself, ‚Äúbut it is in the cold and careless tone which ill suits the word. His warhorse‚ÅÝ‚Äîhis hunting hound, are dearer to him than the despised Jewess!‚Äù

‚ÄúMy mind, gentle maiden,‚Äù continued Ivanhoe, ‚Äúis more disturbed by anxiety, than my body with pain. From the speeches of those men who were my warders just now, I learn that I am a prisoner, and, if I judge aright of the loud hoarse voice which even now dispatched them hence on some military duty, I am in the castle of Front-de-Boeuf‚ÅÝ‚ÄîIf so, how will this end, or how can I protect Rowena and my father?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe names not the Jew or Jewess,‚Äù said Rebecca internally; ‚Äúyet what is our portion in him, and how justly am I punished by Heaven for letting my thoughts dwell upon him!‚Äù She hastened after this brief self-accusation to give Ivanhoe what information she could; but it amounted only to this, that the Templar Bois-Guilbert, and the Baron Front-de-Boeuf, were commanders within the castle; that it was beleaguered from without, but by whom she knew not. She added, that there was a Christian priest within the castle who might be possessed of more information.

‚ÄúA Christian priest!‚Äù said the knight, joyfully; ‚Äúfetch him hither, Rebecca, if thou canst‚ÅÝ‚Äîsay a sick man desires his ghostly counsel‚ÅÝ‚Äîsay what thou wilt, but bring him‚ÅÝ‚Äîsomething I must do or attempt, but how can I determine until I know how matters stand without?‚Äù

Rebecca in compliance with the wishes of Ivanhoe, made that attempt to bring Cedric into the wounded Knight‚Äôs chamber, which was defeated as we have already seen by the interference of Urfried, who had also been on the watch to intercept the supposed monk. Rebecca retired to communicate to Ivanhoe the result of her errand.

They had not much leisure to regret the failure of this source of intelligence, or to contrive by what means it might be supplied; for the noise within the castle, occasioned by the defensive preparations which had been considerable for some time, now increased into tenfold bustle and clamour. The heavy, yet hasty step of the men-at-arms, traversed the battlements or resounded on the narrow and winding passages and stairs which led to the various bartisans and points of defence. The voices of the knights were heard, animating their followers, or directing means of defence, while their commands were often drowned in the clashing of armour, or the clamorous shouts of those whom they addressed. Tremendous as these sounds were, and yet more terrible from the awful event which they presaged, there was a sublimity mixed with them, which Rebecca‚Äôs high-toned mind could feel even in that moment of terror. Her eye kindled, although the blood fled from her cheeks; and there was a strong mixture of fear, and of a thrilling sense of the sublime, as she repeated, half whispering to herself, half speaking to her companion, the sacred text‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄúThe quiver rattleth‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe glittering spear and the shield‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe noise of the captains and the shouting!‚Äù

But Ivanhoe was like the warhorse of that sublime passage, glowing with impatience at his inactivity, and with his ardent desire to mingle in the affray of which these sounds were the introduction. ‚ÄúIf I could but drag myself,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúto yonder window, that I might see how this brave game is like to go‚ÅÝ‚ÄîIf I had but bow to shoot a shaft, or battle-axe to strike were it but a single blow for our deliverance!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîIt is in vain‚ÅÝ‚Äîit is in vain‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI am alike nerveless and weaponless!‚Äù

‚ÄúFret not thyself, noble knight,‚Äù answered Rebecca, ‚Äúthe sounds have ceased of a sudden‚ÅÝ‚Äîit may be they join not battle.‚Äù

‚ÄúThou knowest nought of it,‚Äù said Wilfred, impatiently; ‚Äúthis dead pause only shows that the men are at their posts on the walls, and expecting an instant attack; what we have heard was but the instant muttering of the storm‚ÅÝ‚Äîit will burst anon in all its fury.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîCould I but reach yonder window!‚Äù

‚ÄúThou wilt but injure thyself by the attempt, noble knight,‚Äù replied his attendant. Observing his extreme solicitude, she firmly added, ‚ÄúI myself will stand at the lattice, and describe to you as I can what passes without.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou must not‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou shall not!‚Äù exclaimed Ivanhoe; ‚Äúeach lattice, each aperture, will be soon a mark for the archers; some random shaft‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúIt shall be welcome!‚Äù murmured Rebecca, as with firm pace she ascended two or three steps, which led to the window of which they spoke.

‚ÄúRebecca, dear Rebecca!‚Äù exclaimed Ivanhoe, ‚Äúthis is no maiden‚Äôs pastime‚ÅÝ‚Äîdo not expose thyself to wounds and death, and render me forever miserable for having given the occasion; at least, cover thyself with yonder ancient buckler, and show as little of your person at the lattice as may be.‚Äù

Following with wonderful promptitude the directions of Ivanhoe, and availing herself of the protection of the large ancient shield, which she placed against the lower part of the window, Rebecca, with tolerable security to herself, could witness part of what was passing without the castle, and report to Ivanhoe the preparations which the assailants were making for the storm. Indeed the situation which she thus obtained was peculiarly favourable for this purpose, because, being placed on an angle of the main building, Rebecca could not only see what passed beyond the precincts of the castle, but also commanded a view of the outwork likely to be the first object of the meditated assault. It was an exterior fortification of no great height or strength, intended to protect the postern-gate, through which Cedric had been recently dismissed by Front-de-Boeuf. The castle moat divided this species of barbican from the rest of the fortress, so that, in case of its being taken, it was easy to cut off the communication with the main building, by withdrawing the temporary bridge. In the outwork was a sallyport corresponding to the postern of the castle, and the whole was surrounded by a strong palisade. Rebecca could observe, from the number of men placed for the defence of this post, that the besieged entertained apprehensions for its safety; and from the mustering of the assailants in a direction nearly opposite to the outwork, it seemed no less plain that it had been selected as a vulnerable point of attack.

These appearances she hastily communicated to Ivanhoe, and added, ‚ÄúThe skirts of the wood seem lined with archers, although only a few are advanced from its dark shadow.‚Äù

‚ÄúUnder what banner?‚Äù asked Ivanhoe.

‚ÄúUnder no ensign of war which I can observe,‚Äù answered Rebecca.

‚ÄúA singular novelty,‚Äù muttered the knight, ‚Äúto advance to storm such a castle without pennon or banner displayed!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîSeest thou who they be that act as leaders?‚Äù

‚ÄúA knight, clad in sable armour, is the most conspicuous,‚Äù said the Jewess; ‚Äúhe alone is armed from head to heel, and seems to assume the direction of all around him.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat device does he bear on his shield?‚Äù replied Ivanhoe.

‚ÄúSomething resembling a bar of iron, and a padlock painted blue on the black shield.‚Äù34

‚ÄúA fetterlock and shacklebolt azure,‚Äù said Ivanhoe; ‚ÄúI know not who may bear the device, but well I ween it might now be mine own. Canst thou not see the motto?‚Äù

‚ÄúScarce the device itself at this distance,‚Äù replied Rebecca; ‚Äúbut when the sun glances fair upon his shield, it shows as I tell you.‚Äù

‚ÄúSeem there no other leaders?‚Äù exclaimed the anxious enquirer.

‚ÄúNone of mark and distinction that I can behold from this station,‚Äù said Rebecca; ‚Äúbut, doubtless, the other side of the castle is also assailed. They appear even now preparing to advance‚ÅÝ‚ÄîGod of Zion, protect us!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîWhat a dreadful sight!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThose who advance first bear huge shields and defences made of plank; the others follow, bending their bows as they come on.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThey raise their bows!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîGod of Moses, forgive the creatures thou hast made!‚Äù

Her description was here suddenly interrupted by the signal for assault, which was given by the blast of a shrill bugle, and at once answered by a flourish of the Norman trumpets from the battlements, which, mingled with the deep and hollow clang of the nakers, (a species of kettledrum,) retorted in notes of defiance the challenge of the enemy. The shouts of both parties augmented the fearful din, the assailants crying, ‚ÄúSaint George for merry England!‚Äù and the Normans answering them with loud cries of ‚ÄúEn avant De Bracy!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîBeau-seant! Beau-seant!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîFront-de-Boeuf √Ý la rescousse!‚Äù according to the war-cries of their different commanders.

It was not, however, by clamour that the contest was to be decided, and the desperate efforts of the assailants were met by an equally vigorous defence on the part of the besieged. The archers, trained by their woodland pastimes to the most effective use of the longbow, shot, to use the appropriate phrase of the time, so ‚Äúwholly together,‚Äù that no point at which a defender could show the least part of his person, escaped their cloth-yard shafts. By this heavy discharge, which continued as thick and sharp as hail, while, notwithstanding, every arrow had its individual aim, and flew by scores together against each embrasure and opening in the parapets, as well as at every window where a defender either occasionally had post, or might be suspected to be stationed‚ÅÝ‚Äîby this sustained discharge, two or three of the garrison were slain, and several others wounded. But, confident in their armour of proof, and in the cover which their situation afforded, the followers of Front-de-Boeuf, and his allies, showed an obstinacy in defence proportioned to the fury of the attack and replied with the discharge of their large crossbows, as well as with their longbows, slings, and other missile weapons, to the close and continued shower of arrows; and, as the assailants were necessarily but indifferently protected, did considerably more damage than they received at their hand. The whizzing of shafts and of missiles, on both sides, was only interrupted by the shouts which arose when either side inflicted or sustained some notable loss.

‚ÄúAnd I must lie here like a bedridden monk,‚Äù exclaimed Ivanhoe, ‚Äúwhile the game that gives me freedom or death is played out by the hand of others!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîLook from the window once again, kind maiden, but beware that you are not marked by the archers beneath‚ÅÝ‚ÄîLook out once more, and tell me if they yet advance to the storm.‚Äù

With patient courage, strengthened by the interval which she had employed in mental devotion, Rebecca again took post at the lattice, sheltering herself, however, so as not to be visible from beneath.

‚ÄúWhat dost thou see, Rebecca?‚Äù again demanded the wounded knight.

‚ÄúNothing but the cloud of arrows flying so thick as to dazzle mine eyes, and to hide the bowmen who shoot them.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat cannot endure,‚Äù said Ivanhoe; ‚Äúif they press not right on to carry the castle by pure force of arms, the archery may avail but little against stone walls and bulwarks. Look for the Knight of the Fetterlock, fair Rebecca, and see how he bears himself; for as the leader is, so will his followers be.‚Äù

‚ÄúI see him not,‚Äù said Rebecca.

‚ÄúFoul craven!‚Äù exclaimed Ivanhoe; ‚Äúdoes he blench from the helm when the wind blows highest?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe blenches not! he blenches not!‚Äù said Rebecca, ‚ÄúI see him now; he leads a body of men close under the outer barrier of the barbican.35‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThey pull down the piles and palisades; they hew down the barriers with axes.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîHis high black plume floats abroad over the throng, like a raven over the field of the slain.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThey have made a breach in the barriers‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey rush in‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey are thrust back!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîFront-de-Boeuf heads the defenders; I see his gigantic form above the press. They throng again to the breach, and the pass is disputed hand to hand, and man to man. God of Jacob! it is the meeting of two fierce tides‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe conflict of two oceans moved by adverse winds!‚Äù

She turned her head from the lattice, as if unable longer to endure a sight so terrible.

‚ÄúLook forth again, Rebecca,‚Äù said Ivanhoe, mistaking the cause of her retiring; ‚Äúthe archery must in some degree have ceased, since they are now fighting hand to hand.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîLook again, there is now less danger.‚Äù

Rebecca again looked forth, and almost immediately exclaimed, ‚ÄúHoly prophets of the law! Front-de-Boeuf and the Black Knight fight hand to hand on the breach, amid the roar of their followers, who watch the progress of the strife‚ÅÝ‚ÄîHeaven strike with the cause of the oppressed and of the captive!‚Äù She then uttered a loud shriek, and exclaimed, ‚ÄúHe is down!‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe is down!‚Äù

‚ÄúWho is down?‚Äù cried Ivanhoe; ‚Äúfor our dear Lady‚Äôs sake, tell me which has fallen?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe Black Knight,‚Äù answered Rebecca, faintly; then instantly again shouted with joyful eagerness‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄúBut no‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut no!‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe name of the Lord of Hosts be blessed!‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe is on foot again, and fights as if there were twenty men‚Äôs strength in his single arm‚ÅÝ‚ÄîHis sword is broken‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe snatches an axe from a yeoman‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe presses Front-de-Boeuf with blow on blow‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThe giant stoops and totters like an oak under the steel of the woodman‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe falls‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe falls!‚Äù

‚ÄúFront-de-Boeuf?‚Äù exclaimed Ivanhoe.

‚ÄúFront-de-Boeuf!‚Äù answered the Jewess; ‚Äúhis men rush to the rescue, headed by the haughty Templar‚ÅÝ‚Äîtheir united force compels the champion to pause‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThey drag Front-de-Boeuf within the walls.‚Äù

‚ÄúThe assailants have won the barriers, have they not?‚Äù said Ivanhoe.

‚ÄúThey have‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey have!‚Äù exclaimed Rebecca‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äúand they press the besieged hard upon the outer wall; some plant ladders, some swarm like bees, and endeavour to ascend upon the shoulders of each other‚ÅÝ‚Äîdown go stones, beams, and trunks of trees upon their heads, and as fast as they bear the wounded to the rear, fresh men supply their places in the assault‚ÅÝ‚ÄîGreat God! hast thou given men thine own image, that it should be thus cruelly defaced by the hands of their brethren!‚Äù

‚ÄúThink not of that,‚Äù said Ivanhoe; ‚Äúthis is no time for such thoughts‚ÅÝ‚ÄîWho yield?‚ÅÝ‚Äîwho push their way?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe ladders are thrown down,‚Äù replied Rebecca, shuddering; ‚Äúthe soldiers lie grovelling under them like crushed reptiles‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThe besieged have the better.‚Äù

‚ÄúSaint George strike for us!‚Äù exclaimed the knight; ‚Äúdo the false yeomen give way?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo!‚Äù exclaimed Rebecca, ‚Äúthey bear themselves right yeomanly‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe Black Knight approaches the postern with his huge axe‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe thundering blows which he deals, you may hear them above all the din and shouts of the battle‚ÅÝ‚ÄîStones and beams are hailed down on the bold champion‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe regards them no more than if they were thistledown or feathers!‚Äù

‚ÄúBy Saint John of Acre,‚Äù said Ivanhoe, raising himself joyfully on his couch, ‚Äúmethought there was but one man in England that might do such a deed!‚Äù

‚ÄúThe postern gate shakes,‚Äù continued Rebecca; ‚Äúit crashes‚ÅÝ‚Äîit is splintered by his blows‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey rush in‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe outwork is won‚ÅÝ‚ÄîOh, God!‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey hurl the defenders from the battlements‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey throw them into the moat‚ÅÝ‚ÄîO men, if ye be indeed men, spare them that can resist no longer!‚Äù

‚ÄúThe bridge‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe bridge which communicates with the castle‚ÅÝ‚Äîhave they won that pass?‚Äù exclaimed Ivanhoe.

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù replied Rebecca, ‚ÄúThe Templar has destroyed the plank on which they crossed‚ÅÝ‚Äîfew of the defenders escaped with him into the castle‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe shrieks and cries which you hear tell the fate of the others‚ÅÝ‚ÄîAlas!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI see it is still more difficult to look upon victory than upon battle.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat do they now, maiden?‚Äù said Ivanhoe; ‚Äúlook forth yet again‚ÅÝ‚Äîthis is no time to faint at bloodshed.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt is over for the time,‚Äù answered Rebecca; ‚Äúour friends strengthen themselves within the outwork which they have mastered, and it affords them so good a shelter from the foemen‚Äôs shot, that the garrison only bestow a few bolts on it from interval to interval, as if rather to disquiet than effectually to injure them.‚Äù

‚ÄúOur friends,‚Äù said Wilfred, ‚Äúwill surely not abandon an enterprise so gloriously begun and so happily attained.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîO no! I will put my faith in the good knight whose axe hath rent heart-of-oak and bars of iron.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîSingular,‚Äù he again muttered to himself, ‚Äúif there be two who can do a deed of such derring-do!36‚ÅÝ‚Äîa fetterlock, and a shacklebolt on a field sable‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat may that mean?‚ÅÝ‚Äîseest thou nought else, Rebecca, by which the Black Knight may be distinguished?‚Äù

‚ÄúNothing,‚Äù said the Jewess; ‚Äúall about him is black as the wing of the night raven. Nothing can I spy that can mark him further‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut having once seen him put forth his strength in battle, methinks I could know him again among a thousand warriors. He rushes to the fray as if he were summoned to a banquet. There is more than mere strength, there seems as if the whole soul and spirit of the champion were given to every blow which he deals upon his enemies. God assoilize him of the sin of bloodshed!‚ÅÝ‚Äîit is fearful, yet magnificent, to behold how the arm and heart of one man can triumph over hundreds.‚Äù

‚ÄúRebecca,‚Äù said Ivanhoe, ‚Äúthou hast painted a hero; surely they rest but to refresh their force, or to provide the means of crossing the moat‚ÅÝ‚ÄîUnder such a leader as thou hast spoken this knight to be, there are no craven fears, no cold-blooded delays, no yielding up a gallant emprize; since the difficulties which render it arduous render it also glorious. I swear by the honour of my house‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI vow by the name of my bright ladylove, I would endure ten years‚Äô captivity to fight one day by that good knight‚Äôs side in such a quarrel as this!‚Äù

‚ÄúAlas,‚Äù said Rebecca, leaving her station at the window, and approaching the couch of the wounded knight, ‚Äúthis impatient yearning after action‚ÅÝ‚Äîthis struggling with and repining at your present weakness, will not fail to injure your returning health‚ÅÝ‚ÄîHow couldst thou hope to inflict wounds on others, ere that be healed which thou thyself hast received?‚Äù

‚ÄúRebecca,‚Äù he replied, ‚Äúthou knowest not how impossible it is for one trained to actions of chivalry to remain passive as a priest, or a woman, when they are acting deeds of honour around him. The love of battle is the food upon which we live‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe dust of the melee is the breath of our nostrils! We live not‚ÅÝ‚Äîwe wish not to live‚ÅÝ‚Äîlonger than while we are victorious and renowned‚ÅÝ‚ÄîSuch, maiden, are the laws of chivalry to which we are sworn, and to which we offer all that we hold dear.‚Äù

‚ÄúAlas!‚Äù said the fair Jewess, ‚Äúand what is it, valiant knight, save an offering of sacrifice to a demon of vain glory, and a passing through the fire to Moloch?‚ÅÝ‚ÄîWhat remains to you as the prize of all the blood you have spilled‚ÅÝ‚Äîof all the travail and pain you have endured‚ÅÝ‚Äîof all the tears which your deeds have caused, when death hath broken the strong man‚Äôs spear, and overtaken the speed of his warhorse?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat remains?‚Äù cried Ivanhoe; ‚ÄúGlory, maiden, glory! which gilds our sepulchre and embalms our name.‚Äù

‚ÄúGlory?‚Äù continued Rebecca; ‚Äúalas, is the rusted mail which hangs as a hatchment over the champion‚Äôs dim and mouldering tomb‚ÅÝ‚Äîis the defaced sculpture of the inscription which the ignorant monk can hardly read to the enquiring pilgrim‚ÅÝ‚Äîare these sufficient rewards for the sacrifice of every kindly affection, for a life spent miserably that ye may make others miserable? Or is there such virtue in the rude rhymes of a wandering bard, that domestic love, kindly affection, peace and happiness, are so wildly bartered, to become the hero of those ballads which vagabond minstrels sing to drunken churls over their evening ale?‚Äù

‚ÄúBy the soul of Hereward!‚Äù replied the knight impatiently, ‚Äúthou speakest, maiden, of thou knowest not what. Thou wouldst quench the pure light of chivalry, which alone distinguishes the noble from the base, the gentle knight from the churl and the savage; which rates our life far, far beneath the pitch of our honour; raises us victorious over pain, toil, and suffering, and teaches us to fear no evil but disgrace. Thou art no Christian, Rebecca; and to thee are unknown those high feelings which swell the bosom of a noble maiden when her lover hath done some deed of emprize which sanctions his flame. Chivalry!‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhy, maiden, she is the nurse of pure and high affection‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe stay of the oppressed, the redresser of grievances, the curb of the power of the tyrant‚ÅÝ‚ÄîNobility were but an empty name without her, and liberty finds the best protection in her lance and her sword.‚Äù

‚ÄúI am, indeed,‚Äù said Rebecca, ‚Äúsprung from a race whose courage was distinguished in the defence of their own land, but who warred not, even while yet a nation, save at the command of the Deity, or in defending their country from oppression. The sound of the trumpet wakes Judah no longer, and her despised children are now but the unresisting victims of hostile and military oppression. Well hast thou spoken, Sir Knight‚ÅÝ‚Äîuntil the God of Jacob shall raise up for his chosen people a second Gideon, or a new Maccabeus, it ill beseemeth the Jewish damsel to speak of battle or of war.‚Äù

The high-minded maiden concluded the argument in a tone of sorrow, which deeply expressed her sense of the degradation of her people, embittered perhaps by the idea that Ivanhoe considered her as one not entitled to interfere in a case of honour, and incapable of entertaining or expressing sentiments of honour and generosity.

‚ÄúHow little he knows this bosom,‚Äù she said, ‚Äúto imagine that cowardice or meanness of soul must needs be its guests, because I have censured the fantastic chivalry of the Nazarenes! Would to heaven that the shedding of mine own blood, drop by drop, could redeem the captivity of Judah! Nay, would to God it could avail to set free my father, and this his benefactor, from the chains of the oppressor! The proud Christian should then see whether the daughter of God‚Äôs chosen people dared not to die as bravely as the vainest Nazarene maiden, that boasts her descent from some petty chieftain of the rude and frozen north!‚Äù

She then looked towards the couch of the wounded knight.

‚ÄúHe sleeps,‚Äù she said; ‚Äúnature exhausted by sufferance and the waste of spirits, his wearied frame embraces the first moment of temporary relaxation to sink into slumber. Alas! is it a crime that I should look upon him, when it may be for the last time?‚ÅÝ‚ÄîWhen yet but a short space, and those fair features will be no longer animated by the bold and buoyant spirit which forsakes them not even in sleep!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîWhen the nostril shall be distended, the mouth agape, the eyes fixed and bloodshot; and when the proud and noble knight may be trodden on by the lowest caitiff of this accursed castle, yet stir not when the heel is lifted up against him!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîAnd my father!‚ÅÝ‚Äîoh, my father! evil is it with his daughter, when his grey hairs are not remembered because of the golden locks of youth!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîWhat know I but that these evils are the messengers of Jehovah‚Äôs wrath to the unnatural child, who thinks of a stranger‚Äôs captivity before a parent‚Äôs? who forgets the desolation of Judah, and looks upon the comeliness of a Gentile and a stranger?‚ÅÝ‚ÄîBut I will tear this folly from my heart, though every fibre bleed as I rend it away!‚Äù

She wrapped herself closely in her veil, and sat down at a distance from the couch of the wounded knight, with her back turned towards it, fortifying, or endeavouring to fortify her mind, not only against the impending evils from without, but also against those treacherous feelings which assailed her from within.
